data.title: Apple Among 15 Companies To Announce Dividend Increases In The Second Half Of April
data.description:
This is the latest in my series of articles where I provide predictions of annual dividend increases for long-term dividend growth companies. At the end of March, I provided predictions for 9 dividend growth companies that have historically announced annual payout increases in the first half of April. In this article I’ll look at another 15 dividend growth companies that I expect will announce their annual dividend increases in the second half of April. Before I get to my predictions for the next two weeks, let's see how I did with my predictions (you can see the article with my original predictions here.)

(All yields are based on stock prices at the market close on Friday, April 17th.)

Results for Dividend Increase Announcements in the First Half of April

AON plc (AON) – 15 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 10.1 – 12.8% increase to $3.28 - $3.36

Actual: 10.1% increase to $3.28

Forward yield: 0.99%

The Irish insurance broker continues to compound its dividend at 10% annually.

American Water Works Co., Inc. (AWK) – 17 years

Prediction: 6.9 – 9.1% increase to $3.54 - $3.61

Actual: Deferred to the 2nd half of April

The New Jersey-based utility should announce its next annual increase when it reports earnings on April 30th.

Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) – 22 years

Prediction: 11.5 – 13.1% increase to $5.80 - $5.88

Actual: 13.1% increase to $5.8

Forward yield: 0.59%

As expected, the wholesale retailer continues to grow its payout at 12-13% annually.

H. B. Fuller Company (FUL) – 56 years

Prediction: 5.3 – 7.4% increase to $0.99 - $1.01

Actual: 4.3% increase to $0.98

Forward yield: 1.52%

Dividend growth continues to slow at the specialty chemical company as this year’s payout increase is down from 5.6% last year.

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) – 64 years

Prediction: 5.8 – 6.9% increase to $5.50 - $5.56

Actual: 3.1% increase to $5.36

Forward yield: 2.29%

While disappointing, the increase from Johnson & Johnson extends its dividend growth streak to 64 years.

Procter & Gamble Co. (PG) – 70 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 3.0 – 5.0% increase to $4.354 - $4.4386

Actual: 3.0% increase to $4.354

Forward yield: 2.96%

The consumer products company hits a milestone of 70 years of dividend growth with this boost.

Southern Company (SO) – 24 years

Prediction: 2.7 – 3.4% increase to $3.04 - $3.06

Actual: Deferred to the 2nd half of April

Atlanta-based Southern Company should announce its next annual dividend increase in the second half of the month.

Sonoco Products Co. (SON) – 43 years

Prediction: 1.9 – 3.8% increase to $2.16 - $2.20

Actual: 1.9% increase to $2.16

Forward yield: 3.76%

This is the third straight year of 4-cent annual dividend growth at the packaging and container manufacturer.

Travelers Companies Inc. (TRV) – 24 years

Prediction: 5.0 – 6.3% increase to $4.62 - $4.68

Actual: 13.6% increase to $5.00

Forward yield: 1.66%

I expected the insurer to focus on share buybacks. And they did, buying back $2 billion of shares during the most recent quarter. But the board also decided to approve the largest dividend boost since 2012.

Predictions for Dividend Increases in the Second Half of April

There are 15 long-term dividend growth companies that I expect to announce annual increases in the first half of April. First, here is my prediction for one featured company:

Apple Inc. (AAPL) – 13 years of dividend growth

The massive consumer electronics company is one of the best-known companies on the planet, with worldwide sales of nearly $450 billion annually. Apple breaks its business segments out geographically into the Americas; Europe, which includes India, the Middle East and Africa; Greater China, including Hong Kong and Taiwan; Japan; and the Rest of the Asia Pacific. The company saw sales grow across all geographic segments, with worldwide sales up more than 6% in 2025. Apple did even better in the first quarter of fiscal 2026, which included the Christmas season – sales were up more than 15% as iPhone sales led the way.

With this rate of sales growth, earnings have followed. Apple’s dedication to buying back shares has also boosted EPS: EPS in 2025 were up 23% to $7.49 and grew another 18% in the first quarter of 2026. So with this level of earnings growth, can investors expect a return to double-digit percentage dividend growth? I think that’s unlikely for two primary reasons. First, as I noted above, Apple has a consistent and aggressive share buyback program. Since 2021, the company has reduced the number of outstanding shares by 10% and since 2017 the share count has dropped by more than 25%. The second reason is that Apple is investing a great deal of free cash flow in R&D, specifically in artificial intelligence research. Apple reported R&D expenses grew 10% in 2025 and by more than 30% in the first quarter of 2026. Apple has established a steady, if low, dividend growth rate of 5% over the last half-decade. With the company using cash flow for share repurchases and R&D, I expect another year of dividend growth in the mid-single digit percentages.

Prediction: 4.8 – 7.7% increase to $1.09 - $1.12

Predicted Forward Yield: 0.40 – 0.41%

And here are my predictions for 14 other long-term dividend growth companies which should announce annual increases in the second half of April:

Company # Yrs Industry Prediction (%) New Annual Rate
Ameriprise Financial Inc. (AMP) 20 Financial - Asset Management 8.8% - 10.0% $6.96 - $7.04
The investment manager continues to grow at double-digits. Ameriprise just announced that it has $1.4 trillion in assets under management, which drove adjusted EPS growth of 14% in 2025. Along with compounding its dividend at 9% annually, the company aggressively repurchases its stock – it’s bought back more than 17% of outstanding shares since 2021. Although last year’s 8% increase was a little lower than normal, I expect this year’s dividend boost to return to the normal 9 – 10% range. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.53 - 1.54%
Avery Dennison Corporation (AVY) 15 Consumer Cyclical - Packaging & Containers 4.3% - 6.4% $3.92 - $4.00
Avery Dennison is a specialized materials company whose business includes self-adhesive labelling and radio-frequency IDs. The company has compounded its dividend by roughly 10% annually over the last decade. But at 7%, last year’s payout boost was below the long-term average. This year, I think we can expect dividend growth to fall further – the company’s sales growth and EPS growth came in at only 1%. Avery Dennison also focuses on share buybacks and has bought back more than 7% of shares in the last 5 years. For these reasons, I expect investors to see a boost in the mid-single digit percentages as dividend growth continues to slow. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.27 - 2.32%
Factset Research Systems Inc. (FDS) 27 Financial - Financial Data & Stock Markets 4.5% - 6.4% $4.60 - $4.68
The financial intelligence and data company continues to consistently grow revenues and earnings, with 2025 revenues up 5% and adjusted EPS up 3%. The company’s looking at a 6% growth in both revenues and adjusted EPS for 2026. With a payout ratio around 25% and a small share buyback program (FactSet has repurchased less than 2% of outstanding shares since 2021), the company is well-positioned for another dividend increase in the mid-single digit percentages, similar to last year’s 6% increase. Predicted Forward Yield:1.98 - 2.01%
First Financial Bankshares, Inc. (FFIN) 15 Financial - Regional Banks 5.3% - 10.5% $0.80 - $0.84
This regional bank saw good EPS growth in 2025 due to a big drop in charge-offs from bad accounts. This more than offset flat account and loan growth. EPS grew more than 13% year-over-year in 2025 to $1.77. With the company’s current dividend of 76 cents, this gives First Financial a healthy payout ratio of 43% and leaves room for another year of dividend growth. I expect the company to continue to grow its annual dividend by 4 to 6 cents. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.46 - 2.59%
W. W. Grainger Inc. (GWW) 54 Industrials - Industrial Distribution 9.7% - 11.1% $9.92 - $10.04
The industrial distributor took a loss in the third quarter when it exited its U.K. business, resulting in EPS falling 8.6% despite 5% sales growth. Ignoring the one-time costs, EPS grew about 1% to $39.48. While the small adjusted EPS growth might be a cause for concern, Grainger is expecting a minimum of 20% EPS growth in 2026. The company is also expecting to repurchase $1 billion in shares, building on the 7% of share repurchases completed over the last 5 years. With the expectations of outstanding EPS growth next year, I anticipate a good 10% increase from the company. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.85 - 0.86%
International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) 30 Technology - Information Technology Services 3.0% - 4.2% $6.92 - $7.00
The technology company has suffered from inconsistent earnings per share, hitting a low of $1.80 in 2022, bouncing back to $8.14 in 2023, and then falling to $6.43 in 2024. With earnings jumping all over the place, IBM chose to hold its dividend growth to 4 cents a year for the last half decade, a rate below 1% a year. Earnings rebounded 74% in 2025 to $11.14 and adjusted EPS grew 12% to $11.59, on the double-digit revenue growth in the Software and Infrastructure segments. But while IBM’s financials are improving, the last several years have taken its toll. I expect the company will accelerate the dividend growth, but only to the 3 – 4% range while the company waits to see if the rebound sticks. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.73 - 2.76%
Lithia Motors, Inc. (LAD) 15 Consumer Cyclical - Auto & Truck Dealerships 7.3% - 9.1% $2.36 - $2.40
The auto and truck retailer continues to grow through acquisitions, including recent purchases of dealerships in Tennessee and Oregon. Prior to last year, Lithia had a history of 10%+ increases; the company broke that streak with a 4% increase as EPS fell for the second year in a row. The company reversed the fall in EPS in 2025 with 10% growth to more than $32 a share. While this might drive the company to return to 10%+ increases, Lithia continues to use its free cash flow to aggressively expand through acquisitions and as well as buying back shares. I expect investors to see an acceleration in dividend growth, but it'll still be below 10%. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.83 - 0.85%
Metlife Inc. (MET) 13 Financial - Life Insurance 4.4% - 5.7% $2.37 - $2.40
While Metlife is primarily an insurance company, the company also manages retirement and pension plans for other businesses. The bulk of the company’s earnings come from two business segments: (1) Group Benefits and Retirement and (2) Income Solutions, the former of which grew net earnings by 5%. Overall, Metlife grew its adjusted EPS by nearly 10% in 2025. However, the company has a consistent dividend growth rate in the 4 – 5% range and uses the rest of its free cash flow to buy back shares. And the company is buying back shares aggressively, to the tune of 20% of outstanding shares since 2021 and 37% since 2017. Given this history, I expect Metlife to boost its payout by about 5% this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 3.04 - 3.07%
MSA Safety Inc. (MSA) 54 Industrials - Security & Protection Services 3.8% - 5.7% $2.20 - $2.24
The manufacturer and marketer of safety equipment posted a loss of 10% in EPS on sales growth of 4% in 2025. Nearly all of the sales growth came from the international market and EPS growth there partially offset a loss in the company’s Americas segment. But business was good enough for MSA Safety to announce a $500 million share buyback program in February. The company also is actively spending some of its free cash flow on strategic acquisitions, so I think that dividend growth will be muted this year, with MSA announcing a mid-single digit percentage increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.27 - 1.29%
Parker-Hannifin Corporation (PH) 69 Industrials - Specialty Industrial Machinery 10.0% - 11.1% $7.92 - $8.00
The industrial engineering company is on the brink of becoming one of only a handful of companies that has grown its dividend for 70 years. Along with a long history of dividend growth, Parker-Hannifin has an excellent compounded growth rate of 11% over the last decade. Investors are likely to see another good payout boost this year – the company grew adjusted EPS by 7% in 2025 and is guiding to 11% growth in 2026 on 6% sales growth. I expect another double-digit percentage increase from Parker-Hannifin. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.80 - 0.81%
Pool Corporation (POOL) 15 Industrials - Industrial Distribution 1.6% - 3.2% $5.08 - $5.16
The aptly named pool supply company has established a record of quickly growing its payout but that’s changed over the last couple of years. After peaking in 2022, EPS have fallen every year since then, hitting $10.85 last year on flat sales. While the company is expecting a bit of a rebound this year, the peak earnings growth guidance of 3% isn’t enough to reverse the slowdown in dividend growth. I expect dividend growth to slow from last year’s 4% increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.23 - 2.27%
Portland General Electric Company (POR) 19 Utilities - Regulated Electric 2.9% - 4.8% $2.16 - $2.20
The data center growth in Portland GE’s area of operations has driven good revenue growth. To meet growing electricity demand, the company recently acquired generation operations from PacifiCorp. Unfortunately, the company is issuing additional shares to fund the acquisition, adding to the share count that’s up 40% since 2023. While the company continues to affirm 5 – 7% long-term EPS growth, adjusted EPS were down 3% in 2025. With adjusted EPS guidance of up to $3.53 for 2026, the company might just about hit the lower end of the 5 – 7% growth guidance, but until the 2026 numbers come in I expect dividend growth to slow from last year’s 5% boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 4.12 - 4.20%
RTX Corporation (RTX) 32 Industrials - Aerospace & Defense 5.9% - 7.4% $2.88 - $2.92
RTX is benefiting from increased military spending, with sales and profits up 18% in the company’s Raytheon segment and in the military portion of the Pratt & Whitney segment. The company just reported full company adjusted EPS up 10% in 2025 and guided to 6 – 7% adjusted EPS growth for 2026. This level of growth is consistent with RTX’s 5-year compounded dividend growth rate of 7.5%. Last year, RTX announced an 8% dividend boost; I think this year, the company will announce a slightly smaller increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.47 - 1.49%
Sysco Corporation (SYY) 56 Consumer Defensive - Food Distribution 2.8% - 4.6% $2.22 - $2.26
The global food distribution company continues to grow sales at a 3 – 4% clip annually, with adjusted EPS growing at the same rate. Sysco reported adjusted EPS of $4.46 in 2025, up 3.5% year-over-year. The company’s also guiding to adjusted EPS growth of 3% in 2026. In addition to paying dividends, Sysco spends its free cash flow on repurchasing shares, buying back more than 6% of its shares since 2021. While Sysco has a 6% compounded dividend growth rate over the last decade, over the last 5 years that growth rate drops to less than 4%. With the drop in earnings now, I expect this year’s boost will be closer to the 5-year growth rate. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.91 - 2.96%

Summary

Despite the relatively low number of companies announcing annual dividend increases in the first two weeks of April, three of them were in the double digit percentages. Insurer Travelers surprised to the upside with a 14% boost, while insurance broker AON and warehouse retailer Costco continued their historical patterns of double-digit growth. And even among the smaller dividend increases, there were two notable events: Dividend Kings Johnson & Johnson and Procter & Gamble extended their dividend growth streaks to 64 and 70 years, respectively.

Looking forward to the latter half of April, I expect most of the increases to be relatively small, in the low to mid-single digit percentages. Perhaps the biggest event will be the anticipated announcement from Apple. Historically the company’s annual dividend increase has bounced between late April and early May, and I expect this year’s increase to be in late April and in the mid-single digits. Other notable increases that I’m expecting include announcements from Dividend Kings Parker-Hannifin, Sysco, MSA Safety, and W. W. Grainger, with Grainger’s and Parker-Hannifin’s boosts to be at or above 10%.

data.title: MercadoLibre Has Gone Nowhere For 5 Years, That Can Change Soon
data.description:
MercadoLibre (MELI) is one of, if not the best, Latin American companies out there. Yet, the stock has only returned around 19% in the past five years after huge gains in the five years before that. But that's the opportunity.

The valuation has now caught up to the growth potential, with a forward PEG ratio not too far away from 1x. Some investors might be worried about the company's investments in growth, which have hurt margins a bit, but I'd argue that MercadoLibre is making the right move. And it's doing what it always does, investing in the future to capture more profits over time. Analysts expect margins to recover next year and in 2028, which can propel the stock higher relatively soon.

Additionally, it's a leader in Latin American e-commerce by far, and its market share is expected to get bigger over time, all while EPS growth is forecast to be in the 28-40% range for the next three years.

For these reasons, I give MELI stock a Buy rating.

The Clear Market Leader, And That Should Continue

According to EMARKETER, MercadoLibre had a 33% share of total retail e-commerce sales in Latin America last year. The closest competitor is Amazon, at 10.7%, and it's not even close. That's despite Amazon entering the Latin American market 14 years ago.

If Amazon can't overtake MercadoLibre in Latin America, that's a testament to MELI's moat. According to an article from the University of Michigan:

Marcos Galperin [MercadoLibre's co-founder and CEO] has credited the success of the MercadoLibre because they built the platform from the ground up and developed a unique, technological and commercial solution designed to meet the distinctive cultural and geographical challenges of operating an online trading solution in Latin America.

Operating an online marketplace in Latin America, where shipping and postal routes are generally less reliable, isn't easy. But MELI built the entire system from the ground up.

They own the marketplace, logistics (Mercado Envios), financial services (Mercado Pago), and they also do lending (20% of 2025 revenues). A tiny fraction of their revenue also goes toward selling physical fintech products.

Each part of the business reinforces the other. A big marketplace helps them attract more buyers and sellers. Their own logistics system helps keep costs lower and make deliveries more efficient, improving the customer experience. Customer data helps MELI's lending division. Physical fintech product sales keep clients in the MercadoLibre ecosystem.

That ecosystem at this scale creates the competitive advantage. Its revenue breakdown can be seen below, with a big chunk (44%) coming from commerce services. And there has been lots of diversification and expansion over the past few years, helping revenues grow 20x from 2018 to 2025.

Market Share Is Expected To Rise

As the pie graph I showed earlier notes, there's a lot of market share still up for grabs since MELI and AMZN made up "only" 43.7% of 2025's market share. The good news is that MercadoLibre's market share is expected to keep rising.

As you can see, MercadoLibre is forecast to grow its market share to 37% by the end of 2028. Recall that as of the end of last year, it was 33%. Amazon's market share will grow as well, but not nearly as quickly. Meanwhile, "other retailers" and "leading retailers" as a whole will lose share.

What To Make Of Falling Margins

If you looked at MELI's 2025 earnings results, you might have noticed that revenue grew by 39% for the full year, while operating income grew by just 22%. Its operating income margin fell from 12.7% in 2024 to 11.1% last year.

Last month, on March 12, JPMorgan (JPM) warned about margin pressures for MELI, which sent shares lower. The company mentioned intense competition in Brazil, particularly with Shopee being okay with sacrificing its margins. JPMorgan believed that margin pressures weren't priced into consensus estimates. It turns out that the company was right about consensus estimates being too high at that point, since they've come down in the past month.

The consensus EPS revision trends on Seeking Alpha show that up until March 31, the EPS estimates for 2026 and 2027 were $57.73 and 79.71, respectively. Those numbers are now $50.57 and $70.57.

But here's my take. In the short term, it's not ideal, but in the longer term, MercadoLibre's investments should help.

The falling margins are deliberate. Specifically, during the Q4-2025 earnings call, EVP and CFO Martin de Los Santos said:

The margin compression reflects our decision to invest in the areas of the business with the greatest long-term growth opportunity, especially shipping and credit card expansion. We remain confident these investments strengthen our ecosystem, deepen our competitive advantages and expand the long-term growth runway in a region where both e-commerce and financial services remain meaningfully underpenetrated.

It's one thing if MELI was spending insane amounts of money just to keep their market share or still end up losing share. But the company is spending well within its means ($1.48B in 2025 FCF) and is set to gain share.

These investments make sense and will strengthen the moat in the longer term while helping maintain its impressive growth rate. Once the benefits start kicking in, potentially in 2027, margins can recover again.

That's already what analysts are forecasting. Its net income margin was 6.9% in 2025. Meanwhile, 2026 revenue and net income are forecast at $38.63 billion and $2.532 billion, respectively, for a 6.55% margin.

Then, revenue and earnings are forecast to increase to $48.019 billion and $3.536 billion, respectively, which would send the net margin to 7.4%. That trend is expected to continue into 2028 as well (8.45% implied net income margin on $57.434 billion in revenue).

The Valuation Looks Good

If you measure MELI using most traditional metrics, then it'll look overvalued. That helps explain the valuation grade of D+ that it gets on Seeking Alpha. While I'm a fan of Seeking Alpha's quant grades, I don't think MELI deserves such a low grade here. The culprit is that many of its valuation metrics are higher than those of the sector median. The sector in question is the consumer discretionary one, which also includes low-growth retailers like Best Buy (BBY) that drag the multiples lower.

Per Finbox, the consumer discretionary sector as a whole has TTM revenue growth of 21.9%, while MELI's comes in at 39.1%. And I assume that the growth rate among more established consumer discretionary companies is lower than 21.9%.

Once you factor in growth expectations, the valuation looks a lot better, as MELI's revenue forecast CAGR is 28.9% for the next two years compared to the sector's 18.1%. My point is that MELI trades at a premium due to its high growth and dominant position, so I wouldn't write it off based on that.

EPS growth is also expected to be strong, which is why MELI gets a B rating for its non-GAAP forward PEG ratio of 1.12x, lower than the sector median of 1.47x.

It's also worth mentioning that its TTM P/E ratio of 46.25x is 33.4% lower than its five-year average.

Now, below, I've included the EPS forecasts for MELI going as far out as Fiscal 2030. I'll preface that by saying I wouldn't really focus too much on those because only two analysts cover the stock that far out, and it's hard to guess what will happen 4-5 years from now. But I included them for reference and to show how low the forward multiple can get if the growth rate stays this high. The 2030 estimate of $182.41 in EPS gets you a forward P/E of 10x by then.

But if we focus on the nearer-term figures with more coverage (2026 to 2028), it's clear that analysts have high expectations. While 2026 EPS growth is forecast at 28.3%, 2027 growth could be 39.6%, so EPS growth is expected to accelerate. That's if the investments start paying off. Ultimately, I believe MELI's competitive strengths will allow it to maintain high growth and maintain an above-average multiple, which should lead to good gains for long-term investors.

After all, the total addressable market is huge, with a combined population of over 500 million, a GDP of $5.5 trillion, and e-commerce penetration in Latin America of just 14%.

What Are The Risks?

There are a few key risks when it comes to MercadoLibre: macro risk (currency exposure, emerging markets risk), competition, and growth expectations. There are also upcoming earnings. Let's talk about these risks.

Obviously, MercadoLibre's Latin American-based business is a risk itself. It mainly operates in Brazil, Argentina, and Mexico. These countries don't have as stable of an economy as the US does. This is especially true for Argentina, which is still struggling with high inflation. The inflation print in March was 32.6% for the country, while GDP growth was just 0.6% in Q4.

Lately, the US dollar has been weak, which is a good thing for MercadoLibre since it earns foreign currencies. But in general, the USD is considered to be a strong currency and is much more stable than those of Latin American countries. That has consistently shown up in the gap between MercadoLibre's FX-neutral growth vs. its reported growth. An example is Q4 2023, when reported growth was 42% vs. FX-neutral growth of 72%.

Competition is another thing to watch out for. While I'm very confident in MELI's competitive positioning, the truth is that competition is still relevant. If there were no competition at all, MercadoLibre likely wouldn't have to invest more and hurt its margins in the short term. I'll continue to monitor market share trends going forward.

Finally, growth expectations. If you strip away the expected growth, the valuation is high, especially for an emerging markets stock. So, any disappointments in growth can send the stock lower quickly because it's quite volatile.

Speaking of something that can send the stock lower quickly, earnings are coming up.

MELI's Upcoming Earnings Are A Short-Term Risk

MercadoLibre is expected to report its Q1-2026 earnings on May 7. EPS is forecast at $9.81, which would be just a 0.67% YoY growth rate. What's interesting is that the growth rate is expected to quickly pick up in the quarters after that, with 19.18% growth expected in Q2 and 60.38% the quarter after. So Q1 can be the "worst" of margin pressures.

Meanwhile, revenue is forecast to reach $8.31 billion in Q1, or 40% higher YoY.

Nonetheless, the high revenue growth paired with no EPS growth suggests that more margin contraction is on the way, and if management doesn't reassure investors that it's temporary, the stock could sell off.

Another thing I want to mention is that MercadoLibre didn't give us any guidance for Q1, making it more likely for a big surprise in either direction. That's likely a reason why the low and high forecasts are so far apart.

If you haven't noticed already, the low forecast for EPS is $7.24, and the high is $11.64. For revenue, the low forecast is $7.58 billion, and the high forecast is $8.66 billion. These are large ranges, showing how much uncertainty surrounds these results.

If you'd rather have clarity, then waiting until after earnings to make a decision can make sense. But personally, I see this as a long-term investment, where one quarter shouldn't make or break the thesis.

The Bottom Line On MELI Stock

MercadoLibre is a strong company with a huge lead over other e-commerce companies in Latin America. I like to buy leaders, especially ones that are growing quickly, taking market share, and are highly profitable. MercadoLibre is all those things and now trades under its average valuation multiples.

This isn't a "safe" stock by any means. It's volatile, margins are falling in the short term, and there's competition. The upcoming earnings report also adds another short-term risk to look out for. But at the end of the day, it's a high-quality company at a reasonable price, and margins are expected to recover.

Therefore, I give MELI stock a Buy rating, and I've recently bought shares myself.

data.title: F.N.B. Corporation: Disciplined Growth Makes Shares Attractive
data.description:
Shares of F.N.B. Corporation (FNB) have been a strong performer over the past year, rallying 42%. It has been a strong 12 months for most banking stocks, as fears about credit losses last April amid tariff announcements never came to pass. FNB (which operates the First National Bank) has delivered especially strong underwriting results, while its capital position is peer-leading. These strengths have left me bullish on shares, reiterating them at a buy when I last covered FNB in January. Shares are up slightly since then, similar to the broader market. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company’s first quarter, F.N.B. Corporation earned $0.38, which was in line with expectations. EPS was up 19% from last year thanks to balance sheet growth and widening margins, while credit quality has been excellent. Deposits averaged $38.4 billion in the quarter, and balances were up 3.8% from last year, a very healthy level given a competitive environment for deposits. This growth was led by strength in money market balances.

There has been a shift away from CDs to these deposit types as they offer greater flexibility for FNB to cut rates should the Fed pivot to a dovish stance, unlike CDs, which are fixed out for a contractual period of time. I was also encouraged to see non-interest-bearing (“NIB”) balances up about $200 million from last year, as these are very accretive to margins. Interest-bearing deposit costs were down 36bps from last year at 2.4%. With more than $7 billion of balances still over 3.25%, I see scope to continue pushing down deposit rates.

On the asset side of its balance sheet, average loans were $34.9 billion, which was up 2.5% from last year. It has a diversified portfolio with a quarter in commercial real estate, a quarter in residential mortgages, 21% in traditional business lending, and then a host of smaller exposures. During the quarter, it completed the sale of $200 million in lower-yielding residential mortgages that had been announced in Q4. FNB can deploy the proceeds into higher-yielding loan categories given its strong capital position. I see capacity to continue shifting away from mortgages.

There has been significant attention on the exposure banks have to private credit and nondepository financial institutions (NDFI), and this focus is likely to linger for some time, given challenges facing that asset class. This is a segment of the credit market that FNB has avoided, instead focusing on its traditional middle-market corporate lending business. This is consistent with its conservative approach to lending, and as such, I do not see FNB facing material losses, even if private asset pressure were to worsen materially.

Within its CRE portfolio, it has diverse exposure, and the average loan is $1 million, limiting losses if any single property faces issues. Multifamily is its biggest exposure, and from a lending perspective, I remain favorably disposed to this segment. While rents have fallen a bit, limited housing supply remains a key support. We have also seen a shift away from remote working that has limited downside in office space. I am comfortable with FNB’s CRE portfolio.

Management said its loan pipeline is nearly at a record level, which should facilitate healthy loan growth in coming months. I saw some risk that the Iran War could lead to some slowdown in lending, just given uncertainty. While a more prolonged oil crisis could still develop, its focus on middle-market banking and the health of its pipeline should insulate FNB from this and enable continued sequential growth. FNB has a 90% loan-to-deposit ratio, which is a very healthy place to run the bank. As such, I expect loan growth and deposit growth to run at similar paces.

Net interest income was down 2% sequentially to $359 million, which can be explained by the fact there were two fewer calendar days in the quarter. FNB’s net interest margin (“NIM”) was 3.25%. This was up 22bps from last year but down 3bps sequentially. While fixed asset repricing has been a tailwind over the past year, the full pull-through of Q4 rate cuts compressed margins sequentially. NIM ended the quarter at a 3.3% pace, and the maturity of higher-cost CDs should support incremental margin expansion over the balance of the year. It also has $1.3 billion of investment securities rolling off at a 3.14% yield, which it should be able to deploy at higher yields.

Turning to credit quality, FNB is a standout. During the quarter, charge-offs ran at 0.18%, similar to where they have been over the past year. It set aside $19 million for credit losses, flat sequentially. Delinquencies stand at 0.74%, down 1bp from last year, as credit quality has remained firm. Allowances for credit losses cover 1.26% of loans, and they are 3.8x nonperforming loans. I view levels above 2.5x as extremely strong, and allowances cover over 7 years of charge-offs. As such, FNB is very well positioned to handle any surprising deterioration in underwriting results.

Beyond strong reserves, FNB maintains an excellent balance sheet with a tier 1 common equity capital ratio (CET1) of 11.4%. Even factoring in all unrealized losses on its securities portfolio, its capital ratio would be 10.8%. This is solidly above the 9.5-10.5% range I believe FNB should operate in. This excess capital position provides capacity to fund ongoing balance sheet growth while also returning capital to shareholders.

Last week, FNB raised its dividend by 8%, and now shares offer a 2.9% dividend yield. Additionally, it boosted its share buyback authorization by $250 million to $300 million. During Q1, it did $35 million of buybacks, and I expect this authorization to run through most of 2027, with management describing buybacks as an “attractive” use of capital. Given its strong capital and reserve posture, I view its dividend as highly secure, and if anything, it could be more aggressive on buybacks.

While there has been a substantial amount of bank M&A over the past year, I do not expect FNB to take part, instead focusing on organic opportunities within its territories. Indeed, during the quarter, FNB won the right to have an exclusive on-campus banking presence at Penn State University, which has 90,000 students and employees. Beyond this, it now will provide treasury management services for the University itself, which is likely to have the more material near-term benefit to results.

Alongside results, the company updated guidance. The company is maintaining its target of mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth. Given Q1 results and ongoing momentum in the business, I continue to view this guidance as credible, even as geopolitical uncertainty poses some risk to loan demand. Management also reaffirmed its NII outlook while noting that it is modelling in 0 rate cuts (from 2 previously). I still see scope for 1 cut later this year, but this would be no more than a $5 million impact and is unlikely to shift results outside of the range. I expect NII to be at least in the upper half of the range.

Given spending associated with the Penn State deal and other expansion efforts, I expect expenses to run at ~$1.02 billion and see some risk of upward drift beyond that. Higher spending to lay the groundwork for faster medium-term growth is not a negative in my view, unlike a spending miss because core operations are costlier. That said, this could be a modest near-term headwind to 2026 EPS

Back in January, I was targeting $1.65-$1.75 in 2026 EPS, which drove my $20.50 price target. I now see FNB earning $1.67-$1.77, reflecting slightly more share buybacks. The core business is performing in line with my expectations, and I continue to see FNB as something of a “hidden gem” in the banking sector. While not the largest bank, its conservative and disciplined approach to growth has left it with a stellar balance sheet and strong credit quality. I believe share should command a 12x multiple, at the higher end of where I value regional banks, given these strengths. I reaffirm FNB as a buy and expect shares to push past $20 as it continues to deliver solid results.

data.title: PDI: Consistent Payouts Will Erode The NAV Further
data.description:
Overview

2026 has been off to a rough start, but it looks like the equities markets are recovering. However, the debt markets may not be in that recovery phase yet, which is why the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) still remains down for the year. During my last coverage of PDI, I downgraded my rating to a hold because of the weakened earnings and poor dividend coverage at the time. Since that last coverage, PDI has released an updated semi-annual report that provides some transparency into the fund's earnings. Therefore, I wanted to revisit PDI to reassess its current valuation, performance, outlook, and dividend utility. I still believe that PDI can be a solid long-term position, but there may be some short-term risks remaining.

When I previously covered the fund, PDI traded at a premium to NAV of 14.3%. Following the pullback in share price, PDI now trades at a much larger premium to NAV of 7.41%. Referring to the red line on the chart below, we can see that PDI now trades at a more reasonable price-to-NAV level relative to its historical ten-year range. Even on a smaller time frame, PDI has traded at an average premium to NAV of 9.24% over the last five years. Therefore, this can be interpreted as an attractive time to begin accumulating shares. However, there's always the chance the fund's premium to NAV declines further.

PDI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 15.2% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. The latest report indicates that the fund is still struggling to support payouts, but I do attribute this weakness to macro conditions rather than some internal flaw of the fund. Still, PDI would certainly benefit from a dividend reduction. However, management has built a reputation for consistent distributions, so a reduction is very unlikely. While this may be great for income investors, it ultimately comes at the cost of the fund's NAV. Unfortunately, positive growth momentum may not continue until interest rates are substantially lowered.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PDI has total managed assets of $11.4B that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The primary goal of the fund is to generate income by getting exposure across a global range of income-producing securities, which is achieved through different debt obligations with varying credit quality. Therefore, it should be clear that PDI's performance will show very little correlation to the equity markets. As the indices like the S&P 500 (SPX) and Nasdaq-100 (NDX) start to recover from their lows and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, PDI isn't structurally built to participate in this recovery.

Instead, PDI's success is ultimately reliant on the health of the debt markets. An environment of healthy borrowing means that PDI can thrive, whereas an environment where borrowers are struggling to repay loans can cause PDI's earnings to decline. Furthermore, the fund provides some exposure to global securities, but the majority of the fund is still allocated to U.S.-focused positions. The portfolio's breakdown by region is as follows:

  • United States: 67.60%
  • United Kingdom: 4.92%
  • France: 4.06%
  • Spain: 2.26%
  • Colombia: 2.24%

Expanding on this diversity, the fund also invests in a range of securities and sectors. For instance, PDI is most heavily allocated to high-yield credit securities at 21.69% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to non-agency mortgages and non-USD developed securities, which accounts for 20.72% and 15.63% of assets, respectively.

Additionally, PDI's portfolio is spread across a diverse range of sectors to help mitigate any sort of concentration risks. However, I think it's worth noting that PDI is most heavily allocated to technology investments at 7.18% of total assets. During a period when the market continues to sell off software investments due to the rising vulnerability to AI, PDI's earnings may have been negatively impacted by this.

Since the fund focuses on debt securities, this introduces a severe vulnerability to fluctuations in the interest rate environment. Ever since the federal funds rate was hiked through 2022 and 2023, PDI has struggled to regain any positive growth. This may be attributed to the portion of the portfolio with long-dated maturities. Securities with a maturity that is several years out will usually share an inverse relationship with interest rates. The fund's breakdown by maturity is as follows:

  • 0-1 Years: 31.70%
  • 1-3 Years: 16.68%
  • 3-5 Years: 22.96%
  • 5-10 Years: 17.16%
  • 10-20 Years: 9.25%
  • 20+ years: 2.25%

Performance Insights & Outlook

Since the time of my last coverage, PDI has released an updated semi-annual report that highlights the fund's performance for the first half of its fiscal year 2026. Net investment income amounts to $0.94 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.17 per share. Assuming the fund captures a similar level of net investment income over the next half of its period, PDI is aligned to generate net investment income that falls on the lower end of its typical range. Net investment income is made up of the dividends and interest income that the fund earns from its positions. So for a fund that prioritizes a high rate of distributions, I would like to see net investment income substantially improved going forward.

The current dynamic means that net investment income doesn't support the distributions on its own. Therefore, PDI is reliant on net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings. Under normal market conditions this would be more acceptable, but since interest rates are elevated, this makes it unlikely that the fund will be able to capture a meaningful level of net realized gains. For instance, PDI was able to capture net realized gains of $2.57 per share back in 2021, which was when interest rates were low, which severely outpaces the most recent period's net realized gains of $0.17 per share.

Additionally, the fund uses leverage to help amplify its earnings. However, leverage also amplifies downside risks if allocated to securities that fall in value. The fund has total effective leverage of 32.07% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$3.46B. This is an aggressive use of leverage during this period of elevated interest rates, and it significantly increases the risk profile. Furthermore, a high cost of debt will directly impact PDI's operating spread between the cost of leverage and the yield collected from positions, which is why net investment income levels may have declined. This overall structure has resulted in the fund's NAV remaining heavily suppressed since the highs of 2021. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $25.23 per share
  • 2022: $19.72 per share
  • 2023: $17.27 per share
  • 2024: $16.82 per share
  • 2025: $16.93 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $16.90 per share

As we can see, the fund's NAV remains heavily suppressed. Higher interest rates increase the interest expenses that borrowers need to pay, which also means that the risk of defaults becomes more relevant. All of these factors combined will ultimately keep the fund's share price suppressed, and I don't see any relief until interest rates trend lower. The challenge is that we don't know how much longer interest rates will remain elevated. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged.

So when looking at the performance of PDI over the last decade, we can see that PDI's share price sits far below the levels prior to 2020. In fact, PDI's share price was able to provide some slight appreciation prior to 2020 and the higher interest rate environment. Due to the weakness of the last few years, the fund's share price has now gone down nearly 34% over the last decade. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 122.7% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the distribution's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions.

If you're a patient long-term investor, accumulation during this weakness may pay off in the future. However, it still comes down to a lower interest rate environment, so there may be more downside risks remaining. For me personally, I would like to see a reversal in NAV momentum before I find the confidence to start a position. A lower interest rate will serve as a catalyst because it will improve PDI's operating spread, reduce the burden of leverage, and cause its positions to rise in value.

Dividend is Consistent but Hurting the NAV

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.2205 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 15.2%. Assuming the fund keeps payouts unchanged for the next twelve months, we can expect PDI to issue an annual payout of about $2.646 per share. However, the same semi-annual report indicates that PDI is already off to a bad start for the first half of its fiscal year. Total earnings have already failed to support the distributions, which continues the fund's streak of paying out more than it earns. For instance, the fund reported total earnings of $1.11 per share while actively paying out $1.32 per share worth of distributions. In fact, the last time PDI was able to support distributions was back in 2021, and the fund has struggled since.

As long as the fund pays out more than it earns, the underlying NAV will continue to decline. A declining NAV is the clearest indication that something needs to be structurally addressed. While I do attribute the weakened earnings to overall macro conditions and the pressure on the debt markets, I think that PDI can easily fix this issue by reducing the payouts. Looking at the payout history below, PDI has maintained a consistent track record of payouts for more than a decade and has avoided any sort of reductions in payouts. Therefore, I would imagine that management does not plan to initiate any sort of cuts so that their reputation for reliable income remains.

However, I would argue that at a 15.2% dividend yield, PDI can initiate a 20% cut to payouts, and the yield would still sit somewhere around ~12.1%, which is still highly competitive amongst high-yield peers. A reduced payout would reduce the fund's pressure to produce a high level of earnings while also improving the underlying operating margins. This may increase the NAV stability over time and slow the rate of long-term price appreciation. This reduction wouldn't have to be permanent since the payouts can always be raised once interest rates are lower and macro conditions improve.

Like I previously stated, I do think that a lot of the issues with earnings just come down to macro challenges. When assessing the long-term trend of the NAV, PDI has struggled since the uncertainty of the pandemic. As we can see below, PDI's NAV remained quite stable before 2020. Once interest rates were cut, the fund's NAV started to trend higher, but this was disrupted when the Fed aggressively hiked interest rates through 2022 and 2023. Ever since interest rates were raised to their ten-year highs, the fund's NAV has struggled to regain any positive momentum.

On a more positive note, the fund's ability to keep payouts consistent means that investors can create their own dividend growth. Despite the lack of any sort of raises, this consistency means that investors can reinvest dividends or add new capital over time so that their annual dividend income grows. To demonstrate this, I ran a backtest of an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. The backtest assumes that no additional capital was added after the initial investment, but it also assumes that all distributions received were reinvested back into the accumulation of shares. By simply remaining invested and reinvesting those distributions, investors would have seen their annual dividend income more than double.

2017 Dividend Income: $9,963

2025 Dividend Income: $25,393

Lastly, investors need to be aware that the distributions received from PDI may not offer tax efficiency. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that 90.20% of the last payout was classified as net investment income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in PDI may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the negative tax implications.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I unfortunately believe that PDI remains a hold at this time. The fund has a clear weakness during this period of elevated interest rates, but it seems like more downside risks remain if rates are reduced. The fund is likely to continue paying out more than it earns, which will exacerbate the rate of the NAV erosion. Furthermore, a scenario where the debt markets worsen can take the value of PDI's positions lower. While the fund has an established history as a consistent dividend payer, I don't think the fund will be able to see any positive growth momentum until interest rates trend lower. So while I do like the fund, I want to see the NAV momentum reverse and some relief to the debt markets before initiating a position.

data.title: IFRA: The AI Mega Theme Rolls On, Caterpillar Leads The Way
data.description:
The Dow Jones Industrial Average is back within earshot of the 50,000 level. Leading the group of 30 US large caps is Caterpillar (CAT). The now-Texas-based Industrials sector name is up 39% so far this year. It’s also the largest position in the iShares US Infrastructure ETF (IFRA).

I was bullish on the fund back in October of 2025. Shares are up 13% since then, dividends included, outperforming the S&P 500 by 7 percentage points. Today, I reiterate a buy rating. Trading about 5 turns cheaper than the Industrials sector, IFRA sports a solid long-term earnings growth rate and a healthy chart. I’ll provide updates on both measures today, along with a fundamental review.

CAT Best DJIA Stock YTD, Largest Holding in IFRA

IFRA +39% YoY

According to the issuer, IFRA "seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of equities of U.S. companies that have infrastructure exposure and that could benefit from a potential increase in domestic infrastructure activities." IFRA offers exposure to companies that are owners and operators, such as railroads and utilities, and enablers, such as materials and construction companies.

IFRA is a medium-sized ETF, now with $3.7 billion in assets under management as of April 17, 2026. That’s up from $3.2 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low to moderate at 30 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about 0.4 percentage points higher than that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum is quite stout at the moment, earning IFRA a B+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Risk also appears sanguine, thanks to a modest realized historical standard deviation and an in-check implied volatility level of 21%.

As far as liquidity goes, IFRA’s average daily volume is north of 275,000 shares, while its median 30-day bid/ask spread is decently tight at just four basis points, per iShares.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar plots across the style box, with exposure to each of the nine boxes. The plurality, 30%, of IFRA is allocated to mid-cap blend. Overall, 30% of the fund is large cap, with just 15% classified as small cap. On net, there’s both effective size and style diversification. Lacking is geographic diversification, as the ex-US position is a mere 4.8%.

On valuation, unlike the S&P 500’s P/E, IFRA’s earnings multiple has expanded since October 2025. It was just below 17x at the time of my previous assessment, and it’s now a stone’s throw from 21x. But with a long-term EPS growth rate of 9.6%, the resulting PEG ratio is by no means extreme.

IFRA: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, Utilities commands the top spot, at more than 40%. That’s a massive overweight compared to the power generation segment’s stake in the overall US stock market. Of course, many of the largest utilities are not the traditional high-yield and low-risk companies.

Rather, they are focused on data centers, with clear ties to the AI mega theme. What’s more, the significant 35% Industrials position is also linked to the AI buildout. I remain bullish on AI, and it appears that this growth theme may now be overshadowing the conflict in Iran and broader geopolitical turmoil.

IFRA: Holdings & Dividend Information

Turning to seasonality, IFRA has typically performed well from April through July. While the price history does not extend very far, there could be some calendar tailwinds as the Q1 earnings season gets underway in earnest.

IFRA: Bullish Q2 Trends

The Technical Take

With a decent valuation, AI-theme allocation, and positive seasonal factors, IFRA’s technical situation leans encouraging. Notice in the chart below that shares are very close to their all-time high. The February peak is within striking distance, although that also means IFRA lags the S&P 500 over the past couple of months.

So, with lackluster very recent relative strength, it’s all the more critical that the bulls take the ETF to new highs soon. I assert that this may happen, as the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

But take a look at the gap that lingers below today’s level. We could see some minor back-and-filling to just below $58. Longer-term support is seen at $55, as that is just about the peak from October 2025 and the precise low this past March. Reflecting on my Q4 2025 article, the $64 target remains in play based on the depth of the November 2024 to April 2025 plunge and subsequent breakout.

IFRA: Rising 200dma, $64 Remains In Play, Downside Gap Lingers

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on IFRA. The cyclical and Industrials-heavy niche ETF remains in a solid uptrend, while its valuation checks out. It’s led by this year’s best DJIA stock, and I expect new record highs soon.

data.title: ILF: Bullish On Latin America, But I Don't Love This Fund's Portfolio
data.description:
After years of dramatic underperformance for emerging markets, including Latin America, the pendulum seems to be swinging in the other direction. 2025 saw emerging market stocks start to break out in general, and Latin American countries take off in particular.

Just take a look at the chart below, which highlights the performance of various Latin America-related ETFs along with the S&P 500 since January 1st, 2025:

Peruvian shares have more than doubled over the past 15 months. Meanwhile, Colombia, Brazil, Chile, and Mexico all have rallied between 75% and 100%. The regional benchmark, the iShares Latin America 40 ETF (ILF) is up 90% over the span. Down far below those, we find the S&P 500 at a 23% gain, and Argentina at a 14% gain over the period. In any case, Latin America has been on a tear recently.

Why Latin America Is Surging

Latin America is not a monolith. While there is solid correlation across the region, each country's local equity market has a different narrative or key factor that tends to drive prices.

Mexico, for example, is a manufacturing-centric economy that relies heavily on its special trade relationship with the United States and Canada. Mexico has benefitted greatly from post-pandemic supply change adjustments as North American firms move their logistics closer to home. Mexico overtook China as the U.S.'s top source of imports in 2023 and has continued to widen its lead since then. Mexico also has a massive tourism and hospitality sector that further differentiates it from its peers. I'm extremely bullish on Mexico and it's the second-largest country weighting in my own portfolio, only trailing the United States.

By contrast, Colombia is an energy economy. Oil and related products tend to make up about half of the country's total exports in any given year, and coal represents another major chunk of the export pie. If the price of oil remains elevated with the recent Middle East disruptions, that'd be a big plus to Colombia in particular.

Chile and Peru tend to rely on mining products. Needless to say, the surge in gold and silver prices could drive a boom in those economies and indeed Peruvian stocks have been on an absolute tear lately. Chile's massive copper and lithium reserves also position it nicely for electrification, EVs, advances in battery technology and so on. If you're bullish on renewables, Chile is an underrated way to get country-level exposure to that theme.

Argentina has several ways to win. It's known for agriculture, and indeed, it is benefitting from the rise of global food prices. The war in Ukraine, in particular, took a significant amount of foodstuffs out of the global marketplace, leaving room for Argentina to take market share while enjoying firmer pricing. Meanwhile, Argentina has ample mineral reserves, and it also could be a major player in energy with sufficient investment going forward. The rise of President Milei's libertarian reform agenda has stamped out hyperinflation in Argentina, and easing of regulation and import controls should unleash the consumer spending and finance segment of the Argentine economy as well.

And finally, there's Brazil, which is a massive country in its own right and has numerous economic drivers. Huge mining enterprises, energy, leading FinTech operations, manufacturing firms, and the list goes on. Brazil has a ton of investable companies and economic sectors which can potentially gain share internationally amid shifting macroeconomic factors.

There's also the favorable trend that Latin American currencies are starting to appreciate versus the U.S. Dollar. The Mexican Peso, for example, bottomed at about 25 to the dollar during the pandemic. It has rebounded to around 17 as of late. For another, the Colombian Peso slumped to as low as 5,100 against the dollar in 2022. This was a gift for folks that lived in Colombia, such as myself, that bought local real estate during the panic. The peso has now recovered to about 3,600 to the dollar as of this writing. We've seen similar currency moves across the region.

These sorts of FX moves can go on longer than you might expect. In the early 2000s, for example, coming out of the prior Latin American economic bust, the Colombian Peso nearly doubled in value against the dollar. The Colombian stock market, as measured in dollars, doubled in 2003, doubled in 2004, and doubled once again in 2005.

You can get absolutely gigantic moves when a long out-of-favor country returns to prominence, as you get EPS growth, multiple expansion, and a major currency kicker all working synergistically.

I'm not particularly bearish on the U.S. Dollar today. That said, you can easily make an argument that the U.S. could see meaningful capital outflows given its challenging long-term fiscal picture in addition to the high level of policy uncertainty that has been occurring in Washington D.C. in recent years. If you're bearish on dollars, Latin America is a relatively cheap market that would outperform in a "sell the U.S." sort of macroeconomic scenario.

And finally, there's an election catalyst here as well. Latin America has been generally swinging toward the right-wing recently. Chile elected a conservative president in a decisive election result late in 2025, ending years of socialist rule there. Several smaller LatAm countries like Ecuador voted for conservatives last year as well. And, back in 2023, the election of Javier Milei set off a sea change in Argentina.

Coming up later this year, both Colombia and Brazil have presidential elections. Those countries currently have left-wing presidents. And, as per betting markets, conservatives are slightly favored to win both races as of this writing. While these two elections are going to be highly competitive, a conservative win in one or both of these countries could further improve regional sentiment.

I'm Bullish Latin America, But I Simply Don't Like This Portfolio

I could go on all day about why I'm upbeat on Latin America. However, I'll stop there. Because, unfortunately, the bull case for ILF begins to unravel the moment you start kicking the tires on the actual ETF.

My first concern comes from country exposure:

ILF is not really a Latin America fund, in my opinion. Rather, it's a Brazil fund with a healthy dollop of Mexico and then a tiny sprinkle of the other nations. At a mere 2% allocation to Colombia and no exposure to Argentina whatsoever, there simply isn't the level of regionwide diversification I'd want in this product. I'm also far more bullish on Mexico than Brazil, and I've often been long Mexico/short Brazil as a pair trade over the years. Mexico's proximity to the U.S. along with its considerably better governance and rule of law make me far more comfortable allocating capital there over the long-term.

And the second issue is in sectors:

Banks and other financials make up fully 37% of the ETF. That's a massive number. And then the combination of materials and energy make up another 36%. The exposure to sectors like consumer discretionary, health care, and real estate are pitifully small.

Personally speaking, I have a large holding in a Latin American healthcare stock. Just look at the demographics and income trends in the region and you'll see there's plenty of room for rising spend and operator profitability. But, if you buy ILF, you aren't getting any meaningful exposure to that trend. Additionally, my portfolio is loaded with Latin America consumer names. If I'm bullish on the region, I want to be all over that rising middle class spending theme. But, ILF mostly misses that in order to be loaded up on banks, oil companies, and mining operations. There's nothing inherently wrong with those latter types of companies, but they generally aren't the ways to get the most torque to a booming consumer economy.

I own about two dozen different Latin American equities. Despite that, I personally own just two of the top ten holdings in the ILF right now. And only one of them, Nu (NU), looks like a particularly enticing pick for 2026. Yet, the top ten holdings in ILF make up more than half of the overall portfolio. This is a heavily market-cap weighted fund meaning its full of stodgy banks and energy companies rather than the smaller nimbler consumer-facing firms which should surge in a real regional economic boom.

ILF Bottom Line

I'm a long-term bull on Latin America. So much so, in fact, that I learned Spanish as an adult and moved to Colombia, where I currently reside with my wife and kids. I've built a life here, in significant part because I believe several leading Latin American countries, including Mexico, Colombia, and Chile are on a sharply ascending trajectory and will offer better lives and economic opportunities in the future. Naturally, I own a lot of equities in these countries as well, as I see values and growth possibilities that are considerably more attractive than the average S&P 500 component.

And, in the shorter-term, there is also reason for a bullish view. Commodity prices are higher, and inflation seems relatively sticky, at least in the metals, energy, and agriculture markets that drive many LatAm countries' exports. The war in Ukraine and this year's events in Iran, in particular, highlight the value of Latin America as a trusted trade partner that is much closer to the United States and less likely to be negatively impacted by overseas geopolitical shocks. Finally, upcoming regional presidential elections could provide another boost to the bull case.

But, I just don't see ILF as the right tool for expressing this view. The fund's 47 basis point expense ratio is alright, and the fund has plenty of assets and trading volume to facilitate easy entry and exit. But I simply don't like the portfolio. It's way too exposed to Brazil compared to the rest of the region. And key sectors like health care, consumer discretionary, and real estate are way too low in their respective sector weightings.

As such, I'm at a hold on ILF. It will do fine in an ongoing Latin America bull market, but you can do much better picking individual equities across the region.

data.title: TG Therapeutics: Flawless Execution And $500 Million Non-Dilutive Capital Bolster Outlook
data.description:
Overview

My last look at TG Therapeutics stock (TGTX) was in January. I upgraded from Hold to Strong Buy. TGTX has risen 17% versus S&P 500 (SPY) gains of 2.7%.

I've covered TG Therapeutics for three years now. As some readers might remember, I was initially quite bearish on the name, but its Multiple Sclerosis [MS] therapy, Briumvi, has wildly outperformed my expectations. In fact, it has secured a healthy niche in the MS market despite much larger competitors.

TG Therapeutics is also developing a subcutaneous [SC] version of Briumvi, which should materially expand its total addressable market, especially as the more convenient SC versions are gaining in popularity.

Recall that I performed a discounted cash flow analysis on TG Therapeutics, modeling for 20% CAGR and free cash flow margins.

Based on these assumptions, TGTX was undervalued by roughly 21% at that time. Since then, its stock has reached my estimated DCF Value per Share of $35.22, and now appears appropriately priced, with expectations largely dependent on TG Therapeutics' ability to get its SC Briumvi to the market.

So I wanted to take another look to see if recent changes are still supportive of investment.

Recent Developments

In late February, TG Therapeutics released its Q4 and full year 2025 results. It set a Q1 2026 Briumvi product revenue target between $185 million and $190 million. TG Therapeutics also maintained its full year operating expense guidance of $350 million in "core operating expenses," plus approximately $100 million associated with the SC buildout.

Regarding intravenous [IV] Briumvi, TG Therapeutics recently completed the enrollment in the Phase 3B ENHANCE trial that consolidates Day 1 and Day 15 IV dosing. Given the amount of similar products on the MS market, the company has to "go out of its way" to differentiate Briumvi. This is, really, the reason for ENHANCE.

In April, TG Therapeutics announced that its Phase 3 SC Briumvi trial was fully enrolled. Because it is already known that Briumvi is effective in MS, this trial is just a matter of making sure that the SC version allows for similar exposure.

TG Therapeutics is also busy advancing its early-stage allogenic CD19 CAR-T, azer-cel. The Phase 1 trial in progressive MS is now adding participants across the U.S., and preliminary data is expected in the second half of this year.

As I noted in my January analysis, azer-cel remains a "long-dated call option." But they plan on potential expansion into other autoimmune indications like myasthenia gravis. I imagine they are waiting for some safety/tolerability data before committing money to other autoimmune conditions.

IV → SC

TG Therapeutics is still very much a "Briumvi story." And despite being approved years ago, the therapy is still gaining steam.

During the call, management cited “record new patient enrollments” and “continued share gains across patient types, sites of care, and academic/community settings.”

They admitted that they were being “somewhat conservative” with their revenue guidance, so this reads like a classic “guide conservatively and beat later.”

Now, there are some headwinds to be aware of in Q1, like seasonal impacts and gross-to-net, but these appear to be accounted for in the guidance.

Mind you, TG Therapeutics does have a history of conservative revenue guides followed by strong beats.

Regarding SC Briumvi for self-administered at-home use, management believes that it could nearly double their total addressable market.

Although they're a little late to the "SC game," its peers with SC treatment options have demonstrated how popular they can be. Novartis' (NVS) Kesimpta, for instance, generated a whopping $4.4B in FY2025 global sales.

Because TG Therapeutics is already marketing IV Briumvi, they can add a SC version into its existing salesforce for little incremental expense. And SC products ordinarily carry much higher margins than IV ones.

Financial Health

TG Therapeutics ended the year with approximately $200 million in cash, and boosted its buyback plans to $300 million. That's another way for management to say that they think the stock is cheap relative to the cash flow ramp they are seeing, which helps support my discounted cash flow/break-even analysis.

TG Therapeutics refinanced an old $250 million facility and drew a new five-year, $750 million term loan. They drew upon the $750 million term loan immediately and pocketed $500 million in net cash, raising its pro forma cash to roughly $700 million.

It's at SOFR plus 4.75%, and amortization only kicks in in early 2030.

The major advantage in financing like this is that it did not require any equity issuance, so no dilution. It's just financial flexibility.

Relative to the 2024 facility, this new one looks more favorable, which suggests a few things.

For one, management isn't raising capital because they need it (they generated $19.7 million in cash from operations last quarter). It looks like they're doing it because the business is scaling faster than the Street fully appreciates, and they also want the "dry powder" for buybacks and business development.

It also shows that Blue Owl is deepening the relationship, so they must also feel increasingly comfortable with TG Therapeutics' long-term prospects.

Risks to Thesis

Some risks to my Buy investment thesis include stifling competition in the IV and SC MS markets, clinical trial failures, revenue misses, and stock underperformance.

In one scenario, SC Briumvi will be entering a market with established SC agents, like Kesimpta. It may be challenging for them to secure market share. I'm also assuming SC Briumvi will become approved. The translation from IV to SC is not automatic. So, Phase 3 data will be telling. Although management has a history of beat-and-raises, should IV Briumvi experience any headwinds (e.g., pricing pressure, market share loss), the company's financial performance could fall short.

Conclusion

TG Therapeutics continues to execute flawlessly since my January rating upgrade. Briumvi IV steady and expanding niche in the anti-CD20 MS market provides a solid foundation, while the SC program, now fully enrolled ahead of schedule, lengthens the franchise. Management is clearly "putting its money where its mouth is" with accelerated share repurchases and now $500 million in fresh non-dilutive capital.

I still feel comfortable with my original discounted cash flow and break-even framework. That may even look more conservative today, although, as I mentioned to start the article, TG Therapeutics stock is more expensive than it was in January.

Given that its price has now caught up a bit, I'm going to "downgrade" my rating from Strong Buy to Buy. I don't mean this as a bad thing, it's mostly "clerical," to recognize that the stock has a more beefy valuation than it did in January. I remain optimistic on the company's prospects.
